text.title: microservices-architecture Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for microservices-architecture as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Two services sharing the same database or the same schema tables.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Schema coupling makes independent deployment impossible. A migration in Service A's table breaks Service B's queries. This produces a distributed monolith with all operational costs but no deployment independence.

failure-mode.description: Distributed monolith: services that must be deployed together because they share libraries, schemas, or runtime state.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: All the operational complexity of microservices (network calls, distributed tracing, multiple deployments) with none of the benefits (independent release cadence, independent scaling). The root cause is missing domain boundaries at design time.

failure-mode.description: Shared database: multiple services reading from or writing to the same database tables.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Schema coupling prevents independent migration and deployment. A column rename in one service breaks another's queries silently. Forces coordinated releases, undermining service autonomy.

failure-mode.description: Too-small services (nanoservices): services so fine-grained that network overhead, deployment complexity, and distributed coordination dominate over business logic.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Each service boundary introduces network latency, serialisation cost, and deployment overhead. Services sized below a bounded context create chatty communication and force distributed transactions for simple operations.

failure-mode.description: Synchronous communication everywhere: every inter-service call is synchronous HTTP, even for operations that do not require an immediate response.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Cascading failures: if Service C is slow, Service B waits, and Service A waits. Async decoupling isolates availability. Synchronous calls should be the exception, reserved for cases where the caller must act on the response before proceeding.

failure-mode.description: No API versioning: services expose a single unversioned API endpoint, breaking clients on any schema change.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: In microservices, services are deployed independently. Without versioning, a schema change in one service forces coordinated upgrades of all consumers — reintroducing deployment coupling that microservices exist to eliminate.

failure-mode.description: Chatty communication: service A makes N+1 calls to service B for a list of N items (one call per item).
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: N+1 calls across a network are orders of magnitude slower than N+1 calls within a process. Aggregation, BFF (Backend for Frontend) pattern, or batch APIs reduce inter-service round trips.
